structured_choices.data_source: Electronic health records (EHR) from a single hospital system spanning 5 years, comprising 10,000+ heart failure patient discharge records. Data includes all encounters from admission through 30-day post-discharge follow-up period. Time period: Years 1–5 of available records, with explicit temporal ordering preserved.
structured_choices.label_definition: Binary outcome: readmission within 30 days of discharge (yes/no). Defined operationally as any unplanned hospital readmission with an admission date ≤30 days after index discharge date. Readmission status is determined from admission records in the same EHR system. Patients lost to follow-up or with incomplete discharge records are excluded from analysis.
structured_choices.split_method: Temporal split (not random). Training set: Years 1–3 (all discharges). Validation set: Year 4 (all discharges). Test set: Year 5 (all discharges). Justification: Readmission prediction is inherently temporal; future readmission patterns may differ from historical patterns due to changing care protocols, patient populations, or external factors. Temporal splitting prevents the model from learning non-causal associations and ensures the test set represents genuinely held-out future data. Within each temporal window, no stratification is applied to splits; stratification on outcome would leak distributional information and is inappropriate for temporal data.
structured_choices.preprocessing_fit_point: All preprocessing transformers (feature scaling, categorical encoding, imputation strategies) are fit exclusively on the training set (Years 1–3). Fitted transformers are then applied identically to validation and test sets without re-fitting. This prevents data leakage from validation and test distributions into the training process. Imputation strategy (e.g., forward-fill for time-series clinical values, mean imputation for static demographics) is determined using only training data.
structured_choices.features: Raw signals are extracted from EHR data at the time of discharge or within 24 hours prior. Features include: (1) Static demographics (age, sex, race, insurance type); (2) Clinical history (prior readmissions in past 12 months, prior ICU stays, comorbidity indices); (3) Admission details (length of stay, admission source, discharge disposition); (4) Laboratory and vital signs (most recent values before discharge: ejection fraction, BNP/NT-proBNP, hemoglobin, creatinine, systolic BP); (5) Medications prescribed at discharge (ACE inhibitors, beta-blockers, diuretics—as binary indicators); (6) Temporal features (day of week discharged, month, seasonality indicator). No features encoding the outcome (e.g., post-discharge intervention dates) are included. Feature engineering is performed on the training set only; any derived features (e.g., ratios, polynomial terms) use statistics computed from training data.
structured_choices.model: Hierarchical attention mechanism neural network (proposed model): A multi-head attention layer applied over sequences of temporal clinical observations (e.g., vital signs and labs over the admission period), followed by a secondary attention mechanism aggregating information across different clinical domains (vitals, labs, medications), feeding into a fully connected classification head. Architecture: embedding layer → hierarchical attention blocks → dense layers with ReLU → output sigmoid. Baseline model: A standard RNN (LSTM with 2 layers, 128 hidden units) using the same input feature sequences, same output task. Both models process sequences of length equal to admission length (variable, padded to max length in training set).
structured_choices.baseline: The LSTM baseline is given equivalent tuning effort: hyperparameter search over learning rate, dropout rate, number of layers, and hidden dimension size is performed using the same validation set and metric (AUC). Both models are trained for the same maximum number of epochs with early stopping on validation AUC. Both use identical feature inputs and preprocessing. The LSTM is a reasonable baseline because it is a standard temporal sequence model for EHR data; hierarchical attention is a proposed refinement of attention-based approaches. The comparison isolates the architectural innovation (hierarchical attention) from confounds like feature engineering or tuning effort.
structured_choices.primary_metric: Area under the ROC curve (AUC). Justification: Stakeholders emphasize reducing unnecessary readmissions while maintaining clinical sensitivity. AUC is threshold-agnostic and reflects the model's ability to rank high-risk patients above low-risk patients across all decision thresholds. This aligns with the operational goal (identifying at-risk patients for intervention) and is robust to class imbalance. AUC does not require choosing a single operating point a priori, which is appropriate when the cost of false positives vs. false negatives is not yet fixed by downstream deployment.
secondary_metrics: Precision-recall curve and area under the PR curve (AUPRC): More sensitive to class imbalance than AUC; useful for understanding performance in the minority class (readmitted patients).
Sensitivity at 90% specificity: Operational metric representing the readmission detection rate at a clinically acceptable false positive rate, useful for care management workflow.
Calibration (Brier score, calibration plot): Ensures predicted probabilities are reliable for risk stratification; a well-calibrated model produces probabilities that match observed frequencies.

Performance stratified by key confounds (insurance type, discharge disposition, age quartile): Detects whether model performance degrades for specific patient subgroups, indicating potential confounding.
structured_choices.model_selection_data: Hyperparameter tuning (learning rate, architecture details, regularization) is performed using the validation set (Year 4) with AUC as the selection metric. The validation set is used to select the best performing model and hyperparameters; early stopping is applied during training when validation AUC plateaus. The test set (Year 5) is held completely separate and used only once, at the end, to report final performance.
structured_choices.test_set_policy: The test set is not touched during any phase of model development, hyperparameter tuning, or feature engineering. No threshold tuning, metric computation, or model comparison is performed on the test set until final evaluation. A single evaluation run is executed on the test set after model selection is complete, and results are reported as the final performance estimate. Test set results are compared against baseline results to quantify the improvement (or degradation) from the hierarchical attention mechanism.
structured_choices.confound_controls: Known confounds (hospital protocols, insurance type, referral patterns, patient demographics) are addressed as follows: (1) Stratified evaluation: Performance is evaluated separately for key subgroups (insurance type, age groups, discharge disposition) to detect if the hierarchical model's advantage (or disadvantage) is uniform or concentrated in specific populations. (2) Sensitivity analysis: If hospital protocols changed during Years 1–5, this would manifest as a distribution shift in the temporal split; we test for this by comparing feature distributions and outcome rates across train/val/test sets. Large shifts would indicate the temporal split is appropriate. (3) Confound recording: All recorded confounds (insurance, demographics) are retained as features in both models to allow the model to learn conditional on these variables. (4) Single-site design: Data comes from a single hospital system, reducing variation from inter-hospital protocols, but limits generalizability—acknowledged as a study scope limitation. Multi-site validation would be required for broader deployment claims.

design_narrative:
## Experiment Design: Hierarchical Attention vs. RNN for 30-Day Readmission Prediction

**Objective:** Test whether a hierarchical attention neural network outperforms a standard LSTM-based RNN in predicting 30-day hospital readmission risk for heart failure patients using EHR data.

### 1. Data Acquisition and Labeling

We will use electronic health records from a single hospital system spanning 5 years, comprising 10,000+ discharged heart failure patients. The dataset includes all clinical observations during the admission period (vitals, labs, medications, notes) and discharge records. The outcome label is binary: readmission within 30 days post-discharge (yes/no), determined by querying the EHR for any unplanned admission with an admission date ≤30 days after the index discharge. Patients with incomplete discharge records or lost to follow-up are excluded. Expected class imbalance (~20–30% readmitted) is acknowledged and addressed in the evaluation strategy.

### 2. Temporal Split Strategy

To respect the temporal nature of the problem and ensure the test set represents genuinely future data, we employ a chronological split:
- **Training set:** Years 1–3 of discharge records
- **Validation set:** Year 4 of discharge records  
- **Test set:** Year 5 of discharge records

This temporal design prevents the model from learning non-causal temporal associations and ensures that validation and test performance reflect realistic deployment scenarios where the model encounters data from a future time period. No random shuffling or stratification across time boundaries is applied.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

Features are extracted at the time of discharge or within 24 hours prior. The feature set includes: static demographics (age, sex, race, insurance type); clinical history (prior readmissions, prior ICU admissions, comorbidity indices); admission characteristics (length of stay, admission source, discharge disposition); physiological measurements at discharge (ejection fraction, BNP/NT-proBNP, hemoglobin, creatinine, systolic BP); discharge medications (ACE inhibitors, beta-blockers, diuretics as binary flags); and temporal features (day of week, seasonality). Sequences of clinical observations during the admission period (e.g., daily vitals and labs) are retained as time-series inputs for the neural models.

All preprocessing transformers (scaling, categorical encoding, missing value imputation) are fit on the training set only and applied identically to validation and test sets. This prevents leakage of validation and test distributions into preprocessing parameters.

### 4. Model Specifications

**Proposed model (Hierarchical Attention RNN):** A neural network with hierarchical attention mechanisms: a multi-head attention layer processes temporal sequences of clinical observations (vitals, labs over admission), a secondary attention mechanism aggregates information across clinical domains, and a fully connected classification head produces a binary readmission prediction. Architecture details: embedding layer → hierarchical attention blocks (with residual connections) → 2–3 dense layers (ReLU activation, dropout) → sigmoid output.

**Baseline model (LSTM RNN):** A standard 2-layer LSTM with 128 hidden units, processing the same temporal sequences, with identical input features and output task. The LSTM baseline represents the current standard for temporal EHR modeling.

Both models process sequences of variable length (padded to the maximum admission length observed in the training set). Both are optimized with Adam and trained for up to 200 epochs with early stopping on validation AUC.

### 5. Model Selection and Hyperparameter Tuning

Hyperparameter search is performed on both models using the validation set (Year 4) with AUC as the selection metric. Search space includes learning rate, dropout, number of layers, and hidden dimensions. The best performing model checkpoint (maximum validation AUC) is selected for each approach. The test set is not accessed during this phase.

### 6. Evaluation Metrics and Performance Assessment

**Primary metric:** Area under the ROC curve (AUC). AUC is chosen because it is threshold-agnostic, robust to class imbalance, and directly reflects the operational objective: ranking high-risk readmission patients above low-risk patients for care management intervention.

**Secondary metrics:**
- Area under the precision-recall curve (AUPRC): More sensitive to the minority class and useful for understanding clinical sensitivity.
- Sensitivity at 90% specificity: Operational proxy for detection rate at acceptable false positive rate.
- Brier score and calibration plots: Ensure predicted probabilities are reliable for risk stratification.
- Stratified performance by insurance type, age group, and discharge disposition: Detect whether improvements are uniform or concentrated in subgroups.

Final evaluation is performed once on the held-out test set (Year 5). Test results are reported for both models, and the difference in AUC (hierarchical attention – baseline LSTM) is the primary quantitative outcome.

### 7. Confound Control and Sensitivity Analysis

Known confounds (hospital protocols, insurance, referral patterns) are addressed by: (1) retaining confound variables as features in both models; (2) evaluating performance stratified by key confound categories to detect heterogeneous effects; (3) examining feature distributions across train/val/test sets to detect distribution shifts indicating protocol changes; and (4) reporting results from a single hospital system, with acknowledgment that multi-site validation is required for generalization claims.

### 8. Success Criteria

The hypothesis is supported if the hierarchical attention model achieves higher test-set AUC than the LSTM baseline, with the improvement being statistically meaningful (e.g., ≥0.03 absolute AUC difference) and consistent across secondary metrics and subgroups. If improvement is limited to specific subgroups, the claim is narrowed accordingly.
